title: Cross-Referenced Decay
hypothesis: Cross-Referenced Decay tests whether losing the connections between archive records—not just the records themselves—creates a more profound sense of archival collapse than losing isolated items.
dataModel: network nodes containing text fragments with bidirectional references; seeded 11-23 records with 1-4 references each, reference integrity as first-class state
recordCountStrategy: seeded variable 11-23 nodes using draft timestamp hash, reference density 15-40% of possible connections
interactionModel: click nodes to reveal text and damage connected references; drag between nodes to create false references that corrupt both endpoints; SVG paths update stroke-dasharray and opacity, node text changes visibility, localStorage persists full reference graph state
stateModel: localStorage stores node visibility flags, reference integrity scores (intact/damaged/severed), false reference map, and corruption timestamps per draft ID
visualLanguage: frayed network graph with dissolving connection lines, nodes with partially redacted text, reference paths that thin and break, false connections rendered in offset color
expectedStructuralChange: Clicking a node must change its text content visibility and reduce integrity of at least one connected SVG reference path; dragging between nodes must create a new reference that permanently alters network topology and writes to localStorage.
